codeReview.summary: The feature correctly adds dedicated schedule and validation-state persistence, but the repository leaves stale WakeAlarmSchedule and ValidationStateRecord rows behind when an alarm is deleted, so the persistence contract is still broken for delete flows.

issues.title: Deleting an alarm leaks its dedicated schedule and validation-state records
issues.description: `deleteAlarm(_:)` and `deleteAlarm(byId:)` only delete the `WakeAlarm` row. The feature introduced `WakeAlarmSchedule` and `ValidationStateRecord` as separate persistence models keyed by `alarmId`, but neither delete path removes those records or clears their relationships. After deleting an alarm, `fetchSchedule(for:)` and `fetchValidationState(for:)` can still return orphaned rows for the deleted ID, which violates the stated contract that saved alarms round-trip through dedicated models cleanly and will accumulate stale records over time.
evidence: DawnLoopApp/Services/WakeAlarmRepository.swift:439-548 still fetches schedule/validation rows by alarmId independently of WakeAlarm existence
DawnLoopTests/WakeAlarmRepositoryTests.swift:548-569 only verifies `deleteSchedule(for:)`; there is no test that deleting an alarm removes associated dedicated records
issues.recommendation: Extend both alarm deletion paths to remove any `WakeAlarmSchedule` and `ValidationStateRecord` rows for the alarm ID in the same transaction, and add repository tests covering delete cleanup for both dedicated models.

summary: Review failed: dedicated persistence was added, but deleting an alarm still leaves orphaned schedule and validation-state records behind.
